Go is an open source programming language that makes it simple to build reliable and efficient software.
The language was designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson.
Go is syntactically similar to C, but with memory safety, garbage collection, and structural typing.

Concurrency is one of the most important features of the Go programming language.
Goroutines are lightweight threads managed by the Go runtime.
Channels are the pipes that connect concurrent goroutines, allowing them to communicate.
The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple communication operations.

A goroutine is a function that is capable of running concurrently with other functions.
To create a goroutine we use the keyword go followed by a function invocation.
Goroutines are extremely cheap when compared to threads.
The stack size of a goroutine starts at only a few kilobytes and can grow or shrink as needed.

Channels can be thought of as pipes through which goroutines communicate.
Similar to how water flows from one end to another in a pipe, data can be sent from one end and received from the other end using channels.
By default, sends and receives block until the other side is ready.
This allows goroutines to synchronize without explicit locks or condition variables.

The sync package provides basic synchronization primitives such as mutual exclusion locks.
The WaitGroup type waits for a collection of goroutines to finish.
The main goroutine calls Add to set the number of goroutines to wait for.
Then each of the goroutines runs and calls Done when finished.
At the same time, Wait can be used to block until all goroutines have finished.

Mutexes provide a locking mechanism to ensure that only one goroutine is accessing a section of code at any given time.
Using a mutex, we can protect shared resources from concurrent access.
The Lock method acquires the mutex, and Unlock releases it.
It is common to use defer to ensure the mutex is unlocked.

Context carries deadlines, cancellation signals, and request-scoped values across API boundaries and between processes.
The WithCancel function returns a copy of the parent context with a new Done channel.
The WithTimeout function returns a copy with a timeout that expires after the given duration.
Programs that use contexts should follow these rules to keep interfaces consistent.

Error handling in Go is done through the explicit return of error values.
The error type is a built-in interface in Go.
Functions that can fail typically return an error as the last return value.
If the error is nil, then no error occurred; otherwise, an error was present.
